dialogue.utterance: Dispatching Unit 5 for possible diabetic emergency at a nursing home in Richmond.
Hello, I'm Nathan. I'm an EMS provider with the city. We received a call about a diabetic emergency. Can you tell me what's going on?
We found her unresponsive at 8:00 this morning. Initial blood sugar was 46. We gave 1 mg of glucagon IM but she didn't react.
Is she conscious now?
No. She first started to react when there was movement during our initial transfer here.
She remained unresponsive then. Started to open her eyes in the ambulance.
And the nurse mentioned she's usually aware and oriented to two, right?
Yes. But today she became oriented only to one.
Spontaneous breathing observed. Airway is patent.
She's breathing. Let me assess her breathing.
Let me check her breathing rate and saturation. We just picked her up.
Her breathing rate is 17.
Any signs of difficulty breathing?
Her skin is cool and damp.
Let me check her pulse. This is going to be real quick.
Her pulse is 105.
Blood pressure is 165 over 75. Elevated.
Her initial glucose reading was 46. That's critically low.
How's the saturation?
Her oxygen saturation was in the mid-90s after being placed on oxygen at 10 L/min.
Could you describe her level of consciousness earlier?
She was unresponsive with an unclear Glasgow Coma Scale according to documentation.
Are you ready for an IV line? We're going to give D10W now.
Her initial glucose reading was critically low, 46 - per records.
I'll place the IV in an extremity vein in her left arm after one attempt.
Pushing 100 ml D10W via IV. She started to react after administration.
Following medical diabetic hypoglycemia protocol 3-8 as per standing orders.
Glucose is now 148 - that's much improved.
Her oxygen saturation is stable in the high 90s without oxygen support now.
Her heart rate slowed to 95 now over the past 20 minutes.
Blood pressure has dropped slightly to 153 over 78, but it's still stable for now.
Does she have any pain currently?
No pain reported. She was in a diabetic crisis but no specific pain was mentioned.
Does she have any drug allergies?

No known drug allergies. 36. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; EMT: Can you explain her medical history? 37. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Partner: She has diabetes, hypertension, coronary artery disease, a history of venous clots, and dementia.

She takes amlodipine (Lotrel), Apixiban , Lantus, Humulin R, and metformin (Glypizide). 39. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: events leading up to the event; Partner: Glucagon was administered but didn't resolve her state so we were called.
I'll now place the ECG monitor on a four-lead system for cardiovascular assessment.
EKG shows sinus rhythm with rare PACs.
IV line in the left arm, functioning properly.
She was placed on a non-rebreather mask at 10 L/min initially due to her low oxygen saturation before transport.
We'll secure her onto the cot. The stretcher team is coming over.
Using a sheet lift and three-person carry now to ensure safety for both her and us. 46. Reassessments; retake vital signs: resp; Partner: Rechecking her respiration at 101 per minute.
Performing four-lead ECG monitoring again. Rhythm shows no significant change.
SpO₂ at 95%, stable and satisfactory now without oxygen support.
Glucose remains at 148 - much improved from before and no need for additional dextrose.
Her blood pressure is still at 153/78, stable for now during transport. 51. Reassessments; retake vital signs: pulse; Partner: Pulse at 95 - consistent and stable.
Let me confirm - is her mental status still improving? Did she awaken at all during the second check?
Yes, she started to open her eyes and briefly spoke in the ambulance. Her mental status is much better now.
What is the patient's name so we can document her chart more clearly?
Her name is Mrs. Angela. She's 83 years old.
What hospital is the preferred destination?
St. Mary's is the preferred hospital.
Transport team, we are rolling out now.
She was moved using sheet lift with no signs of distress during the process.
She is secured on the stretcher. Oxygen mask remains off at her current saturation.
Maintaining IV fluid, no additional medications given at this time.
Glucose remains steady at 148.
Any changes in her reported pain, or any new discomfort?
No pain reported from her side, she seems alert and gradually regaining function.
Still cool and damp from the beginning of the interaction. No changes observed.
EKG monitoring was re-established at 08:28 for a 4-lead, still working now.
She is secured and stable at this time for transport. No additional restraints required.
No re-dose of D10W required, she's responding well with current fluid rate. 69. Reassessments; retake vital signs: resp; Paramedic: Respiratory rate unchanged at 101. Continue observation.
No new arrhythmias detected or relevant findings. Recheck showing the same pattern.
Confirmed transport status. En route to St. Mary's.
She is secured on the stretcher. Oxygen mask remains off at her current saturation.
Heart rate still at 95, no significant changes from recent minutes.
Check skin integrity - no signs of burns, breakdown, or irregularities.
No change in pupil appearance or extraocular movement from earlier assessments.

dialogue.role: nose, mouth and throat exam; EMT
dialogue.utterance: No unusual sounds or drainage. Oral airway remains clear.
Clear bilateral breath sounds.
She is secured and stable at this time for transport. No additional restraints required.
Patient's current glucose is 148. No delayed effect from initial hypoglycemic episode. Well within normal range.
No further medications required - oxygen can be discontinued at this point.
Transport team, we're a few minutes out. Continue monitoring, let me know if any changes occur.
Arriving at St. Mary's Hospital in 3 minutes - all going smoothly thus far.
Blood pressure still stable at 153/78. Maintains good peripheral circulation post-treatment.
No signs of dropping again, stable at 148.
Still cool to touch - this is expected following hypoglycemic condition and normal perfusion.
Oxygen saturation now at 95% without oxygen support, no signs of respiratory compromise.
EKG still shows sinus rhythm with rare PAC, no signs of acute cardiac event in our readings to date.

She is awake and slightly oriented. No agitation or signs of distress during transport. 89. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: pertinent past medical history; Partner: Noted CAD and a history of venous embolism in her medical chart from the nursing home.
She was moved using a sheet lift method with no reported complications.
Continued close observation with no changes noted in neurological status during the assessment.
Transport team, we are preparing for arrival. Patient is stable and improving continuously.
No new concerns reported in the past ten minutes. No interventions required since last check.
Final set of vitals unchanged since last reassessment - no clinical deterioration observed.
We'll be arriving in one minute. Prepare for patient handover to St. Mary's ER team.
